description: This site is located in the Peregrine Beach section of Noosa National Park, an area characterised by diverse, low-laying heathland and wallum. Here acid frogs, such as the wallum sedge frog & wallum froglet are at home. Wallum heathland ecosystems occur with acidic, nutrient-poor soil, are very diverse and are currently on the decline due to urban growth.

This site is located in Dulong, an area that is situated within the hinterland, with many farms coexisting with native forest. Here ground dwelling frogs can be found amongst the leaf litter, including the noisy great barred frog.

This site is in Eerwah Vale, an area within the hinterland that features many vegetation types, including dry eucalypt forests and remnant rainforests. At this site, you’ll hear tree frogs croaking from the canopy, and ground frogs scurrying through the leaf litter.

This site is in Yaroomba, a site that combines wallum woodland, rainforest and wetlands. Nestled within the Yaroomba Bushland Park, this site features a large diversity of both acid frogs around the wallum, and tree frogs hiding in plain sight.

This site is in Sippy Downs, this site is the closest to urban areas, but still is home to a lot of different frog species. If you have a keen ear, you’ll hear striped marsh frogs clicking amongst the leaf litter. This site shows how the abundance of frogs can still be high, even in developed areas.

This site is in the Maroochydore Botanic Garden, a well maintained area of eucalypt woodland, rainforest patches and plenty of native flowers. Here ground frogs will be rummaging throughout the leaf litter, with tusk frogs and striped marsh frogs hiding under your feet.
This site is in Maleny, a site that follows along the Obi Obi boardwalk. Here, great and giant barred frogs are in abundance, singing and hunting along the Obi Obi river.

This site is within the Mooloolah Nation Park, a revegetation project organised by the Sunshine Coast Council as an offset to the airport expansion. This site has been revegetated with wallum healthland, and has been a successful ongoing project. Now there is a high abundance of acid frogs living amongst the vegetation.
